response:
The fare calculation involves a base charge for the first four kilometers and extra costs for any distance beyond that limit. We can express the relationship between the fare $\eta$ and the driving distance $\xi$ by noting that $\eta$ equals ten yuan when $\xi$ is less than or equal to four. For distances greater than four the formula becomes ten plus two times the ceiling of the difference between $\xi$ and four. This mathematical model helps us understand how spring weather might influence traffic conditions and thus the total cost. The rounding rule ensures that even a small fraction of a kilometer adds a full two yuan to the final bill.

A passenger who paid thirty-eight yuan traveled a total of fifteen kilometers so we must determine the maximum possible stop time. The base fare covers the first four kilometers leaving thirty-eight minus ten or twenty-eight yuan to cover the excess distance. Dividing twenty-eight by the rate of two yuan per kilometer shows that the charged distance was fourteen kilometers beyond the initial four. This means the total charged distance was eighteen kilometers while the actual travel was only fifteen kilometers. The difference of three kilometers represents the time converted into distance at a rate of one kilometer per five minutes. Therefore the taxi stopped for at most fifteen minutes during this journey in the spring season. Such calculations are vital for understanding how spring rains can extend travel times and increase fares. The driver must account for these variables to provide accurate estimates to passengers.
